mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of the Elastic Stack on Kubernetes. It simplifies the orchestration of Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic components, making it ideal for users who want to leverage Elastic's capabilities in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in managing custom configurations.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install the ECK operator.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the ECK operator.
> Installs the ECK operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Create an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: mmapfs
EOF

```

> Creates a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: mmapfs
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system

```

> Lists the Elasticsearch resources to verify the cluster status.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if Elasticsearch clusters are not deleted properly.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
This step includes backing up your Elasticsearch data, upgrading the operator, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system -o yaml > backup-elasticsearch.yaml

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Elasticsearch cluster is still running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system -o yaml > backup-elasticsearch.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Elasticsearch cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the required permissions are not set for the operator.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the operator has the necessary permissions by checking the Role and RoleBinding configurations.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the Elasticsearch nodes are not starting due to resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check the resource requests and limits in your Elasticsearch CRD and adjust them as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Kibana'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the Kibana service is not properly configured or the Elasticsearch cluster is down.

**Fix:**
Verify the status of the Elasticsearch cluster and check the Kibana configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to create PersistentVolumeClaim'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if there are insufficient storage resources available in the cluster.

**Fix:**
Check your storage class and ensure that there are enough resources available for the PVCs.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.13, ensure that your Elasticsearch configurations are compatible with version 9.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'Green' when running `kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system`.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: mmapfs
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources.
